baseline.basis: Estimated from model memory of industry patterns — a human MUST verify each against the current editions before use. (1) Verizon DBIR-style action-vector prevalence: use of stolen credentials has been the #1 or #2 initial access action in web-app/system-intrusion breaches for several years (~24-38% of breaches involving credential abuse in recent editions), and secrets-in-repos / CI-token abuse features repeatedly in the System Intrusion pattern. (2) GitGuardian State of Secrets Sprawl-style findings: order of 10M+ new hard-coded secrets detected annually on GitHub, with historical private-repo leakage near-universal in orgs of this size and age; Meridian explicitly confirms historical .env/connection strings and a long-lived Contributor SP secret, so the exposure precondition is already TRUE, not probabilistic. (3) Infostealer prevalence (IBM X-Force / Recorded Future / Flashpoint-style reporting): infostealer log volume grew sharply 2023-2025; unmanaged, local-admin, no-EDR laptops give a per-device annual infection probability that, across ~60 engineers with only Defender AV defaults and ~40% outside Intune, makes at least one dev-laptop infection in 12 months more likely than not (rough chain: P(>=1 relevant infostealer/token theft) ~35-60% x P(attacker monetizes PAT into repo mining + Azure auth) ~30-50% x P(result crosses the material threshold, near-certain given 500k PII records reachable) ~80-95% => ~10-25% central). (4) Real-world analogues: CircleCI Jan-2023, Heroku/Travis OAuth 2022, and the 2024 waves of automated GitHub PAT/secret harvesting show this exact chain is actively exploited at scale, not theoretical. Mode 20% reflects that the chain needs an attacker to connect PAT->history->Azure; high 40% reflects fully automated secret-harvesting pipelines making that connection cheap; low 8% reflects the possibility that current infostealer targeting misses this org and stale secrets are non-live.

effectiveness.basis: The adjusted package attacks every link of the named kill chain rather than one: OIDC federation deletes the reusable CI secret (the single highest-leverage step — the scenario's pivot from PAT to subscription simply ceases to exist), history sweep + rotation + Postgres network lockdown deadens the already-leaked secrets, push protection stops replenishment, RBAC downscope caps blast radius below the material threshold for many residual paths, and immutable backups convert the destruction/ransom branch into a recoverable (possibly sub-material) event. Mode 70% rather than higher because of explicitly retained residuals: (a) the endpoint root cause is untouched — infostealers still land, and a stolen GitHub session cookie still yields source code and any bespoke tokens secret scanning misses; (b) feasibility cuts reduce coverage: SAML SSO may be descoped to 2FA+PAT policy on GitHub Team, Entra-auth-to-Postgres deferred to a Key Vault-password fallback (rotated but still a stealable secret), and quick-win #4 (Postgres network lockdown) is gated behind the R5 contractor-access work and may land late or partially; (c) operational drift on a 3-person team: alert-channel fatigue, exception re-grants of Contributor after a broken deploy, missed rotations — assume detective controls operate at perhaps 50-70% of paper effectiveness, business hours only; (d) historical-secret triage is acknowledged as 1-2 person-weeks and will plausibly leave a tail of unrotated low-obviousness credentials. High 85% (not >90%) assumes near-complete execution of all four phases before month 5 with the pilot/parallel-run rollout preventing rollback-to-Contributor; even then the infostealer->session-cookie->source-code path and phished-Entra-portal path keep residual scenario probability materially above zero. Low 45% is the package stalling at ~Phase 1-2 (the reviewer's '40% done' failure mode) — though even Phase 1 alone (OIDC + downscope) removes the worst branch, which floors effectiveness well above zero.

key_assumptions: A 'material incident' (>$25k IR cost or a notification duty) is near-automatic once the production PostgreSQL data or backups (500k end-user PII across 1,200 customer orgs) are accessed by an unauthorized party — notification duty drives materiality more than direct cost.

Baseline reflects ONLY the R3 pathway (externally stolen CI/CD/cloud secrets), excluding overlapping risks (phished Entra accounts, departed-insider credentials R5, endpoint ransomware) even though they share preconditions; the downstream model must not double-count shared events across the five risks.

At least one exploitable live secret currently exists in repo history or GitHub-accessible state (the scenario facts state this; if a sweep found zero live secrets, the baseline mode would drop materially).

Per-year probability that at least one of ~60 engineer laptops (local admin, ~40% un-enrolled in Intune, Defender AV defaults, no EDR) suffers an infostealer/token-theft compromise is assumed in the 35-60% range — this is the least-verifiable number in the chain and dominates the baseline.

Attacker monetization rate of a stolen GitHub PAT into Azure compromise (30-50%) assumes current automated secret-harvesting tooling; if the org's tokens end up in bulk logs actively parsed for cloud credentials, this could be higher.

Effectiveness assumes the ADJUSTED plan: all four phases implemented and operated for the full year, including the Key Vault-password fallback for Postgres (NOT Entra-auth), possible descope of GitHub SAML SSO to 2FA+PAT policy, and quick-win #4 landing only after contractor access is re-homed.
No re-grant of subscription-level Contributor persists after deploy-breakage incidents; the pilot/parallel-run rollout in the adjusted plan is assumed to prevent permanent rollback.

Detective controls (Defender for Cloud plans, Activity Log/sign-in alerts) operate at roughly 50-70% of nominal effectiveness given business-hours-only triage by a 3-person IT team after the 2-4 week tuning period.

Historical secret triage leaves a small tail of unrotated, low-visibility credentials; these are assumed mostly deadened anyway by the Postgres network restriction and blob shared-key changes once those land.

Endpoint hardening (Intune to 100%, EDR, removing local admin) is a SEPARATE risk's mitigation and is NOT assumed here; if it lands in the same year, observed residual risk will be lower than this estimate implies and credit must not be assigned to R3's package.

Baseline and effectiveness ranges are calibrated for a US-based B2B SaaS threat/regulatory environment circa 2025-2026; industry-report figures cited are from model memory (knowledge cutoff Jan 2026) and may misstate current-edition numbers.

The GitHub org is assumed to be on a plan where at minimum enforced 2FA, classic-PAT disablement, fine-grained PAT policy, and org-wide secret scanning with push protection are achievable within the stated budget.
raw_estimates.human_review_reason: All numeric anchors come from LLM memory, not verified sources. A human must: (1) check current-edition Verizon DBIR credential-abuse/System-Intrusion prevalence, IBM Cost of a Data Breach initial-vector shares, and GitGuardian secrets-sprawl figures against the values implied in the basis text; (2) validate the dominant chain input — annual infostealer compromise probability for a ~60-dev, local-admin, no-EDR fleet — against current threat-intel (this single number moves the baseline mode by 2-3x); (3) replace the assumed exposure with ground truth by actually running the gitleaks/trufflehog sweep — if zero live secrets are found the baseline drops sharply, if many are live it rises; (4) confirm the materiality mapping (that DB/backup access triggers notification duty under the applicable jurisdictions for the 500k data subjects); (5) confirm which feasibility descopes actually happen (GitHub plan/SSO, Postgres Entra-auth deferral, quick-win #4 gating) since effectiveness was estimated against the adjusted, not original, package; and (6) ensure the portfolio model does not double-count the shared infostealer precondition across R3, R5, and the endpoint/identity risks.

baseline.basis: Drawn from model memory of industry patterns — a human MUST verify against the current editions before use. Patterns relied on: (1) Verizon DBIR-style findings that credential abuse and phishing are consistently the top two initial action vectors in breaches (~60-70% of breaches involve a human element; stolen credentials appear in roughly a third to half of breaches), and that BEC/ATO is the most frequent incident class for SMBs; (2) Microsoft Digital Defense Report / Entra telemetry-style findings that AiTM (Evilginx-class) kits are now commodity PhaaS (Tycoon 2FA, EvilProxy, Mamba) and that token-theft/AiTM incidents grew sharply since 2022, specifically because OTP/push MFA is phishable — this org's exact configuration; (3) IBM Cost-of-a-Data-Breach patterns that phishing and stolen credentials are the two most common initial vectors, with SMB incidents routinely exceeding the $25k materiality threshold once IR, forensics, and notification duties (500k end-user PII in scope via SharePoint/SSO apps) are counted; (4) FBI IC3-style data that BEC remains the highest-loss crime category, with median wire-fraud losses well above $25k. Reasoning to an annual probability: 200 remote employees receiving commodity AiTM lures, push MFA that a proxied login defeats by design, no Conditional Access, no SIEM, no EDR-driven identity signal, and a 3-person IT team means per-campaign success compounds over 12 months. Low (15%) assumes below-average targeting and some luck; mode (30%) reflects a typical year for an unhardened 200-seat M365 tenant where at least one successful ATO escalates to material BEC or data-download; high (55%) reflects the plausible case that multiple ATOs occur and at least one crosses the materiality line. Note this is probability of a MATERIAL incident (> $25k IR or notification duty), not probability of any successful phish — the latter would be near-certain.

effectiveness.basis: The primary control is architectural, not probabilistic: origin-bound FIDO2/passkey assertions enforced via Conditional Access authentication strengths make Evilginx-class AiTM structurally unable to complete authentication — there is no session cookie to steal. Against the named threat (AiTM credential phishing), a fully-enforced passkey policy is near-total, which is why the HIGH end reaches 92% (above-90 justification: for the users actually covered by the enforced CA policy, the specific attack path in the risk statement is eliminated by protocol design, per CISA/NIST characterization of phishing-resistant MFA and Microsoft's own AiTM guidance; the layered controls — legacy-auth block, device-bound sessions, ID Protection auto-revocation, XDR attack disruption, ATO runbook — also degrade adjacent variants). Effectiveness is capped below 100 and the mode held at 80% because of the feasibility review's realities: (a) coverage — expect ~90-95% durable passkey enforcement, with exclusions, break-glass accounts, new-hire windows, and the 10-12 week rollout leaving months of partial enforcement inside the 12-month window; (b) the Temporary Access Pass help-desk path becomes the residual social-engineering surface, run by the same two overloaded sysadmins; (c) the compliant-device layer is explicitly deferred (Intune 60% -> ~95% moved to the R4 workstream), so cookie/token theft via endpoints remains partially open and interim mode is only block-download; (d) any of the 15 SSO apps where local password login is not actually disabled bypasses every CA control — now in scope but execution-dependent; (e) detection is Microsoft-automated with business-hours-only human response, so a novel evasion can run overnight; (f) operational drift over a year (policy exclusions accreting, alert fatigue in a shared mailbox). LOW (60%) is the case where rollout stalls near the pilot phases, refusal rates run high, TAP issuance is lax, and several SSO apps retain local logins — the controls then mostly protect the privileged/finance cohort while ordinary-user ATO paths persist. This estimate is reduction in the probability of a MATERIAL incident from this risk, not reduction in phishing attempts.

key_assumptions: A 'material incident' means IR cost >$25k or a legal notification duty; given ~500k end-user PII records reachable via SharePoint/SSO and vendor-payment fraud exposure, almost any successful ATO that reaches data download or wire fraud is assumed to cross that threshold.

Baseline percentages are annual probabilities synthesized from model memory of DBIR, Microsoft Digital Defense Report, IBM Cost-of-a-Breach, and FBI IC3 patterns as of the model's knowledge cutoff (Jan 2026); the current-year editions have NOT been checked and figures are calibration judgments, not published rates for this exact profile.

Meridian receives ordinary commodity phishing pressure for a 200-seat B2B SaaS (no evidence of targeted APT interest); a materially higher threat profile would raise both baseline and the value of the mitigation.

The adjusted mitigation is assumed fully implemented AND operated for a full year per the prompt — but 'full year' is interpreted per the feasibility review's own timeline: full CA enforcement lands around month 4, so roughly one-third of the 12-month window has only quick-wins plus privileged-cohort protection.
Passkey enforcement durably covers >=90% of the 200 staff, with break-glass accounts, exclusions, and new-hire/lost-device windows kept small and monitored.

The TAP issuance procedure with live identity verification is actually followed by the help desk under pressure; TAP social engineering is the assumed dominant residual path and is not separately quantified.

SSO-only enforcement (local password login disabled) is verified on all ~15 downstream SaaS apps as the feasibility review moved into scope; each app where this is skipped materially voids the effectiveness estimate for that app's data.

The compliant-device CA layer runs only in 'app-enforced restrictions / block download' mode within this package (Intune gap closure belongs to R4), so endpoint-resident token/PRT theft (R4 territory) is excluded from this risk's scope and NOT counted against R1 effectiveness.

Microsoft-native detection (Entra ID Protection risk policies, Defender XDR attack disruption, DfO P2 Safe Links) performs at typical published efficacy against commodity AiTM kits, with the caveat that attack-disruption signal breadth is reduced until R4's Defender for Endpoint rollout — partially degraded detective value is baked into the mode.

Alert response is business-hours only (written risk acceptance for no 24/7 coverage per the feasibility review); overnight dwell of a few hours before automated containment is assumed survivable without crossing materiality in most cases.

The E5 Security add-on purchase receives CFO sign-off; if it slips, only the $0-license core (passkeys + CA auth strengths + quick wins) operates, which would push effectiveness toward the low end (~60%).
Vendor-side BEC (supplier tenant compromised sending real invoices) is out of scope for R1 and not counted against either number.

Baseline and effectiveness are for THIS risk in isolation; downstream deterministic model handles residual computation and any correlation with R4 (endpoint compromise) — no double-counting adjustments were made here.
raw_estimates.human_review_reason: All quantitative figures are LLM calibration judgments from model memory, not measured rates. A human must: (1) verify the cited industry patterns against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR, Microsoft Digital Defense Report, IBM Cost of a Data Breach, and FBI IC3 — especially phishing/credential-abuse prevalence and AiTM/PhaaS growth claims, which may have shifted since the model's Jan 2026 cutoff; (2) sanity-check the baseline mode (30%/yr material incident) against any available broker/cyber-insurance actuarial data for 100-500 employee SaaS firms with phishable MFA, since no public source reports this exact conditional probability and it is the single most influential judgment call; (3) validate the >90% high-end effectiveness claim by confirming the org actually enforces the phishing-resistant authentication-strength CA policy tenant-wide (not report-only), that TAP issuance verification is operating, and that local-password login is disabled on all 15 SSO apps — the estimate collapses toward the 60% low if any of these silently fail; (4) confirm the scope boundary with R4: endpoint token theft was excluded from R1's effectiveness on the assumption the downstream model treats it under R4, so double-counting or gaps between R1/R4 must be checked at the aggregation layer; (5) review the assumption that partial-year enforcement (full CA by month 4) is compatible with the prompt's 'operated for a full year' framing — if the model should assume day-1 full operation, mode effectiveness could reasonably be 5 points higher.

baseline.basis: Drawn from model memory of industry patterns and MUST be human-verified against current report editions: (a) Verizon DBIR-style action-vector prevalence — 'Basic Web Application Attacks' and 'System Intrusion' together consistently account for roughly a third of confirmed breaches, with exploitation of vulnerabilities as an initial-access vector rising sharply in recent editions (Log4Shell/MOVEit-era pattern of mass exploitation of internet-facing apps); (b) DBIR/CISA observations that internet-facing services are scanned within minutes-to-hours of exposure and that median time-to-exploitation of newly published CVEs has compressed to days; (c) SMB incident-rate surveys (Hiscox Cyber Readiness, Ponemon/IBM SMB studies) suggesting ~20-45% of SMBs report a material cyber incident per year across ALL vectors — R2 is only one vector, so its slice must be well below that; (d) IBM Cost-of-a-Breach patterns showing multi-tenant PII datasets of ~500k records comfortably exceed the $25k IR/notification materiality bar once any tenant table is dumped, meaning almost any successful exploitation here is 'material'. Company-specific aggravators pushing toward the upper range: no WAF, no dedicated security engineer or secure-SDLC gates, mature codebase with unknown SQLi/IDOR debt, no EDR/SIEM so exploitation goes undetected (long dwell converts attempts into material outcomes), shared database where one flaw crosses 1,200 tenants. Moderators pulling toward the lower range: material breach requires an actually-exploitable flaw to exist AND be found — a modern framework app may resist commodity SQLi, and the mode of 20% reflects that uncertainty. The 40% high reflects the plausible world where the years-old codebase has at least one injectable endpoint or a critical framework CVE goes unpatched for weeks with 100+ probes/year against it.

effectiveness.basis: Percent reduction of the R2 baseline assuming the FEASIBILITY-ADJUSTED package is fully implemented and operated for a year. Reduction stack: managed WAF in Prevention mode with origin lockdown removes the bulk of commodity mass-scanner SQLi/known-CVE exploitation (the stated TEF>100/yr driver) — managed OWASP rule sets historically block a large majority of unmodified commodity payloads; Dependabot+CodeQL with a 7/30-day patch SLA shrinks the window in which a published framework CVE is exploitable; least-privilege app role + statement_timeout plus RLS Phase 1 converts many would-be full-tenant dumps into sub-material partial reads (reduces the probability the incident crosses the $25k/notification materiality bar, which counts as reduction under this metric); Defender for App Service/OSS-RDBMS + pgAudit alerting cuts business-hours dwell from months to hours, truncating some exploitations before mass exfiltration; annual pen test + retest removes some IDOR/logic flaws once per year. Capped well below 90% because of explicitly retained gaps: (1) IDOR and business-logic flaws — a named top driver of this multi-tenant risk — bypass both WAF and CodeQL and are only probed annually; (2) RLS is Phase 1 only (top 10-20 tables), allowed to slip past the Type I, and is undermined by contractor direct-DB accounts until the R3 package lands; (3) no nights/weekend response — a Friday-night exploitation still runs a full weekend; (4) phased rollout means alert-only SDLC gates for the first 30 days plus a large pre-existing vulnerability backlog on a burn-down plan, i.e., known criticals stay live for weeks-to-months; (5) operational drift is likely: WAF per-route exclusions accumulate, alert fatigue in a 3-person team with no on-call, pgAudit scoped to ddl+role classes initially so mass-read detection leans on Defender heuristics alone; (6) novel encodings and SSRF-to-metadata patterns partially evade managed rules. Low end (45%) is the world where the dominant residual threat is targeted IDOR/logic abuse (which this package barely prevents) and RLS coverage stalls; high end (80%) is the world where commodity SQLi/CVE exploitation was the overwhelming share of baseline probability and the WAF+patching layers absorb it.

key_assumptions: A 'material incident' is one triggering IR cost >$25k or a breach-notification duty; given ~500k end-user PII records across 1,200 orgs in one shared PostgreSQL database, essentially any successful tenant-data exfiltration via the app is assumed material.

Baseline percentages are annual probabilities of a MATERIAL incident from THIS vector only (app-layer exploitation), not any attempted scan/probe, and not the credential/pipeline vector (R3) — attempts number in the hundreds per year but conversion to material breach is the quantity estimated.

All industry figures cited in the basis fields are from model memory (training data through early 2026) and may misstate current DBIR/Hiscox/IBM edition numbers; they are calibration anchors, not citations.

The application is a mature (years-old) codebase on a mainstream framework with unknown but plausibly nonzero SQLi/IDOR debt; no recent pen test or SAST baseline exists to condition on, so the baseline mode carries wide uncertainty in both directions.

The App Service is currently reachable directly on the internet with no WAF, no virtual patching, and effectively no exploitation detection (no EDR on servers is moot for PaaS, but no SIEM/alerting means dwell is months), so a successful exploit is assumed to run to full exfiltration at baseline.

Effectiveness assumes the feasibility-adjusted plan as written: WAF reaches Prevention mode after ~4 weeks tuning and stays there (no permanent rollback to Detection after a false-positive incident), origin lockdown actually blocks *.azurewebsites.net bypass, and the SDLC gates move from alert-only to blocking on NEW criticals after 30 days.

RLS Phase 1 (top 10-20 highest-sensitivity tenant tables) ships within the year; the 2-day schema/pooling spike does not reveal a blocker that kills the RLS control entirely. If RLS is abandoned, effectiveness mode drops roughly 10 points because the materiality-reduction leg disappears.

The pre-existing Dependabot/CodeQL critical backlog is actually burned down over weeks-to-months by engineering; if the backlog is exempted and ignored, the patching leg contributes far less than assumed.
Detection value is business-hours only; the estimate already discounts for uncovered nights/weekends and assumes leadership accepts (rather than fixes) that residual within the year.

Contractor direct-PostgreSQL accounts and long-lived pipeline secrets (R3 scope) remain in place; their existence limits the blast-radius credit given to RLS. If R3's contractor-account cleanup lands first, true R2 effectiveness would sit toward the high end.

The annual pen test occurs in month ~3-4, findings are remediated, and it finds a representative (not exhaustive) share of IDOR/logic flaws; between tests these flaws are essentially unmitigated on the prevention side.
No major architectural change (new public endpoints, new file-upload surfaces, major framework migration) occurs during the year that would reset WAF tuning and reopen the attack surface.
Baseline and effectiveness are treated as independent of the other four risk packages except where explicitly noted (R3 dependency); no credit is taken in R2 for identity or endpoint mitigations.
The downstream deterministic model combines baseline and effectiveness (residual = baseline x (1 - effectiveness)); no residual math was performed here, per instructions.
raw_estimates.human_review_reason: LLM-derived quantities are the least trustworthy artifact in this pipeline. A human must verify: (1) the industry anchors against CURRENT editions — Verizon DBIR web-app-attack and vulnerability-exploitation prevalence, Hiscox/Ponemon SMB annual incident rates, IBM Cost-of-a-Breach per-record and notification-cost figures — since all numbers here are from model memory and may be stale or misremembered; (2) the baseline mode of 20%/yr against any company-specific evidence that would move it sharply (results of the first ZAP baseline scan, initial Dependabot critical count, any prior pen-test or bug-bounty findings, WAF-detection-mode hit logs once available — these are direct observations that should override this prior); (3) the assumption that essentially any successful app-layer exfiltration is material given US state / GDPR-equivalent notification duties for the actual end-user population; (4) the effectiveness mode of 65%, especially the split between commodity (WAF-addressable) and targeted IDOR/logic (largely non-addressable) threat share, which is the single biggest lever in the estimate and is not empirically grounded; (5) the RLS-ships-this-year and backlog-burn-down assumptions with the Head of Engineering, since the feasibility review itself flags both as at-risk; and (6) the R3 dependency — if contractor direct-DB accounts are not cleaned up, cap effectiveness credit for the RLS control. Recommend re-estimating after the 4-week WAF detection-mode data and the initial CodeQL/Dependabot backlog counts exist, as those two artifacts will shrink the uncertainty bands more than any further desk analysis.

baseline.basis: Drawn from model memory of industry patterns — MUST be human-verified against current report editions. Anchors: (1) Verizon DBIR-style findings that ransomware/extortion appears in roughly a third to ~44% of analyzed breaches and is disproportionately concentrated in SMBs, with system-intrusion via malware/loaders on endpoints a top action vector; (2) Sophos State of Ransomware-style surveys reporting ~50-60% of mid-market orgs experiencing a ransomware ATTACK annually, of which a minority convert to material encryption/extortion events — implying a mid-single-digit to low-teens annual MATERIAL-incident base rate for an average SMB; (3) NetDiligence/Coveware SME claims data showing ransomware as the largest driver of cyber-insurance claims with typical IR costs well above the $25k materiality threshold, so nearly any successful encryption event here is material; (4) IBM Cost-of-a-Breach patterns indicating longer dwell and higher impact where there is no EDR/SIEM coverage. Meridian sits well below the survey-average control baseline for this specific vector: ~40% of laptops unmanaged, users as local admins, default AV with nobody watching alerts, no EDR/SIEM, remote-first personal/work mixing, and OneDrive/SharePoint sync providing a direct encryption path to shared corporate data. That argues for 2-3x the all-SMB average material-incident rate for this single vector: low 8% (if malvertising/infostealer exposure happens to be light and Defender defaults quietly block most commodity loaders), mode 15%, high 30% (if infostealer telemetry-style data on unmanaged-admin endpoints is taken at face value). This estimate is for the endpoint-malware-origin ransomware path only; phished-cloud-session ransomware is counted under R1 to avoid double-counting.

effectiveness.basis: The adjusted package attacks every stage of the R4 kill chain: initial execution (ASR rules, SmartScreen, PUA blocking, tamper protection), privilege escalation (local-admin removal with EPM budgeted as mandatory), lateral/impact (compliant-device CA gating plus the week-1 app-enforced-restrictions quick win that immediately removes sync-encryption for unmanaged devices), detection (Defender for Business EDR with AIR on Full — industry evaluations consistently show managed EDR converting a large majority of commodity loader detonations into contained events), and materiality (tested OneDrive Files Restore/SharePoint versioning turns successful encryption of synced libraries into same-day rollback, often keeping the incident under the $25k/notification materiality bar even when prevention fails). Discounts applied for realism: enrollment plateaus at ~93-97% not 100% (executive stragglers, BYOD dead-ends flagged by the feasibility review); engineer local-admin exceptions persist during and after rollout; 4-5 month calendar means only ~7-8 months of the 12-month window are fully protected; Autopilot zero-touch rebuild was descoped, so recovery of the device fleet is slower than designed; AIR-Full false-positive friction may cause quiet tuning-down of automation; no SIEM means dwell beyond the 30-day SharePoint restore window defeats the corrective control; contractor/BYOD devices and any macOS share are outside the package; business-hours-only triage leaves weekend head starts; data exfiltrated pre-rollout or pre-encryption remains double-extortion leverage that can trigger notification duties even with perfect rollback. Mode 65%; low 45% if enrollment stalls, engineers keep admin en masse, or the calendar slips past the month-4 hard deadline and pieces get descoped; high 80% if the full package operates cleanly for the protected months. Capped below 90% deliberately: partial-year coverage, exception pockets, exfiltration-based extortion, and no-SIEM dwell risk make >90% unjustifiable for this vector.

key_assumptions: Baseline percentages are annual probabilities of a MATERIAL incident (IR cost >$25k or a notification duty), not of any attack or attempted intrusion; given a 3-person IT team and shared SharePoint libraries, almost any successful endpoint-origin encryption event at Meridian is assumed material.

R4 is scoped strictly to the endpoint-malware foothold (trojanized installers, malvertising, infostealers on laptops); ransomware or extortion originating from phished cloud sessions (R1), contractor database access (R3/R5), or Azure-side production assets is excluded here to prevent double-counting across the 5 risk estimates.

Industry base rates cited (DBIR action-vector prevalence, Sophos-style SMB ransomware attack/conversion rates, NetDiligence/Coveware SME claim patterns, IBM CoB dwell findings) are recalled from model memory as of the training cutoff and are assumed directionally correct; a human must check them against current editions before these numbers are used.

The ~2-3x uplift over the all-SMB average material-incident rate for this vector is a judgment call justified by: 40% unmanaged endpoints, universal local admin, default AV with zero alert monitoring, no EDR/SIEM, and OneDrive sync as a built-in blast-radius amplifier.

Effectiveness assumes the ADJUSTED package (12 person-weeks, EPM mandatory, Autopilot descoped, cohort-phased CA, 4-week report-only) is fully implemented AND operated for the year, including the permanent ~3 hrs/week triage rota and monthly compliance attestation actually happening.

Intune enrollment is assumed to reach ~93-97% of in-scope corporate laptops, not 100%; a small executive/BYOD/contractor remainder persists but is confined to browser-only no-sync access by the app-enforced-restrictions policy from week 1.
The fleet is assumed Windows-dominant; a meaningful macOS share would reduce effectiveness at the low end and extend the calendar (feasibility review flags this as a week-1 check).

Defender for Business AIR remains set to Full and is not quietly downgraded after early false-positive isolations of remote workers' laptops; the un-isolation SLA procedure is assumed to exist and work.

The 4-5 month rollout calendar means the effectiveness figure is a blended 12-month number: roughly the first third of the year runs at quick-win-only protection (app-enforced restrictions, tamper protection, ASR on the enrolled 60%), with full protection only for the remaining months.

SharePoint/OneDrive point-in-time restore (30-day window) is assumed sufficient because EDR detection keeps dwell short; if an attacker dwells past 30 days (no SIEM, business-hours triage), the corrective layer fails and only the preventive layers count.

Double extortion via data exfiltrated from an endpoint before or despite the controls can independently trigger a notification duty (PII of ~500k end users may be cached/synced locally); rollback does not neutralize this, which is a main reason effectiveness is capped at 80% high.
Defender for Business eligibility holds (headcount stays under the 300-seat cap) and CSP pricing does not force a downgrade of the EDR component.

Engineer local-admin exceptions are managed via EPM rather than a broad standing exception group; if the exception group swallows the control (feasibility reviewer's stated failure mode), realized effectiveness drops toward the 45% low.

No compensating or aggravating changes outside this package occur during the year (e.g., R1 phishing-resistant MFA landing earlier would slightly lower this baseline; a workforce expansion of unmanaged contractors would raise it).
raw_estimates.human_review_reason: These are LLM-memory estimates, not measured rates, and must be human-verified before the downstream model consumes them. Specifically verify: (1) the industry base rates against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR, Sophos State of Ransomware, NetDiligence Cyber Claims Study, and IBM Cost of a Data Breach — the recalled figures (ransomware in ~1/3-44% of breaches; ~50-60% SMB attack exposure; mid-single-digit to low-teens material-incident conversion) may be stale or misremembered; (2) the 2-3x below-average-maturity uplift, which is analyst judgment with no citable source — a human should sanity-check it against cyber-insurance underwriting benchmarks for orgs lacking EDR/MFA-strength controls; (3) the R1/R4 scope boundary, since double-counting the phishing-origin vs malware-origin ransomware paths across the 5 risks would inflate the aggregate; (4) the effectiveness discount structure — especially the assumptions that enrollment reaches ~95%, AIR stays on Full, and the 4-5 month calendar holds despite SOC 2 consuming ~20% of the same two sysadmins; if the month-4 hard deadline slips and descoping occurs, use the 45% low, not the mode; (5) the materiality threshold mapping — confirm that a same-day Files Restore rollback with no exfiltration would genuinely stay under $25k IR cost for this org, because that assumption does significant work in the corrective control's contribution to effectiveness.

baseline.basis: Drawing on (from model memory — a human MUST verify against current editions): Verizon DBIR-style patterns where use of valid/stolen credentials is the top action vector (~across recent editions roughly 30-40% of breaches involve valid credentials, and Privilege Misuse remains a persistent single-digit-to-low-teens pattern share); Ponemon/DTEX-style Insider Threat reports showing insider incidents (negligent + malicious) are common in mid-market firms and that departing-employee data theft is one of the most frequent malicious-insider forms; industry surveys (e.g., OneLogin/Beyond Identity-style) repeatedly finding that a large share of organizations (often reported as ~half or more) discover ex-employees with still-active accounts, and ~10-25% of surveyed firms attribute an actual incident to a former employee's lingering access; credential-stuffing prevalence against SaaS logins (Akamai/Verizon web-app patterns). Company-specific aggravators pushing above generic base rates: contractors hold standing non-SSO Postgres logins to the crown-jewel dataset (500k end-user PII, so almost any successful export is 'material' via notification duty); six unlinked revocation points with ticket-driven offboarding and no reconciliation; zero detection (no SIEM/EDR, no dormant-account alerting), so a dormant credential can be used for months unnoticed; phishable MFA and historical connection strings in repos make credential reuse plausible. Mitigators keeping the mode single-digit: only ~60 engineers plus a modest contractor pool means few departures/year with production-grade access; a material incident still requires both a missed revocation AND a motivated actor (disgruntled leaver or external attacker finding the credential) in the same 12 months. Low=3% assumes few risky departures and no disputes; high=20% reflects the survey-based frequency of ex-employee-attributed incidents plus the total absence of detection (incidents that would be near-misses elsewhere become material here).

effectiveness.basis: The adjusted package directly attacks the dominant kill chain (missed revocation -> standing Postgres/SSO access -> undetected use): Entra-only Postgres auth makes one account-disable kill production data access (removes the single worst exposure), the leaver runbook/Lifecycle Workflow plus weekly HR-roster-to-Entra/GitHub/pg_roles reconciliation closes the 'missed ticket' path, and dormant-account/post-departure sign-in alerting converts silent long-dwell access into detected-and-revoked events that mostly stay below the material threshold. Layered preventive+detective+quarterly-review design justifies a mode of 65%. Capped well below 90% because of the feasibility scope cuts and residual gaps: (1) the automation trigger (employeeLeaveDateTime) is manually maintained with no HRIS connector — the weekly reconciliation is the real safety net and has up to a 7-day window during which a hostile leaver retains access; (2) pre-departure exfiltration by a soon-to-leave insider is explicitly untouched, and part of the baseline scenario (contractor exports tables after a dispute) can occur before revocation; (3) SCIM cut from ~15 apps to 4-6, the rest stay on a manual checklist — the same human failure mode as today for those apps; (4) GitHub Enterprise deferred: PATs and deploy keys survive until expiry, weekly script-based removal only; (5) app-layer connection strings and .env secrets (usable by anyone who copied them) are out of scope (R3/R4); (6) alert-fatigue/operational drift risk on a 3-person interrupt-driven team even with the named rotation — a year of operation typically sees some decay. Low=45% assumes the manual trigger fails often and alerts become wallpaper; high=80% assumes the reconciliation script and detection run cleanly all year and Postgres Entra-auth cutover reaches all human accounts.

key_assumptions: A 'material incident' is one with IR cost >$25k or a notification duty; because the Postgres database holds PII on ~500k end users across 1,200 customer orgs, any successful export of customer tables is assumed to trigger notification and therefore materiality — this makes severity-given-occurrence high and drives the baseline upward.

Employee/contractor turnover is assumed at roughly 10-20% annually (~10-25 departures/year with some production-grade access, including several contractors with direct Postgres logins); materially different turnover changes both baseline and effectiveness.

Baseline assumes offboarding currently fails partially or fully for a meaningful fraction of departures (consistent with 'manual/ticket-driven' across six systems and industry survey findings), and that no one would detect dormant-credential use for months (no SIEM, no dormant-account alerting).

Industry statistics cited (DBIR credential-misuse prevalence, privilege-misuse pattern share, Ponemon insider-incident frequencies, ex-employee-access survey figures) are recalled from model memory, may be misremembered or superseded, and MUST be checked against the current published editions before these numbers are used.

Effectiveness assumes the ADJUSTED (feasibility-reviewed) scope: phased rollout, SCIM for only 4-6 priority apps, GitHub Enterprise deferred (weekly API reconciliation + PAT lifetime policy instead), budget licensing path, escalate-instead-of-auto-remove for production groups — not the original full package.

Effectiveness assumes the package is fully implemented AND operated for the full 12 months, including: all human Postgres local passwords actually dropped/expired (no stragglers), the weekly HR-roster reconciliation script running and acted on, the alert-triage rotation actually staffed, and quarterly access reviews completed.
HR reliably communicates contract end dates and terminations to IT within days; the weekly reconciliation bounds — but does not eliminate — the exposure window from a missed notification.

Pre-departure insider exfiltration and reuse of application-layer secrets (connection strings, .env files in repos) remain in R5's residual because they are explicitly out of this package's scope (R3/R4); a portion of the baseline scenario (disgruntled leaver exports before departure) is therefore not mitigated here.

No double-counting with other packages: baseline is estimated against CURRENT controls only (phishable MFA, no Conditional Access); if the MFA/Conditional Access package from R-other lands first, both baseline and the marginal effectiveness of R5 would shift.
Credential-stuffing/reuse against dormant accounts is assumed plausible (push-MFA-less Postgres logins, historical secrets in repos), not merely theoretical.
Distribution semantics: low/mode/high are parameters for a downstream PERT/triangular-style fit of annual probability; they are judgment-based calibration points, not measured frequencies.
raw_estimates.human_review_reason: All figures are LLM judgment calibrated against statistics recalled from memory, not retrieved sources. A human must: (1) verify the cited industry patterns against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR (valid-credential and Privilege Misuse pattern prevalence), Ponemon/DTEX insider-threat reports, and any ex-employee-access survey relied on — the recalled percentages may be wrong or outdated; (2) replace the assumed turnover rate with Meridian's actual departure count and the actual number of contractors holding direct Postgres logins, which dominate the baseline; (3) confirm the materiality mapping — whether an export of customer tables genuinely triggers notification duties in the relevant jurisdictions (this single assumption moves the baseline several points); (4) validate the effectiveness ceiling against the final implemented scope (was the Postgres Entra-auth cutover completed for ALL human accounts? is the weekly HR reconciliation actually running?), since the estimate assumes the adjusted, not original, package; (5) check for interaction with the MFA/Conditional Access package to avoid double-counting risk reduction across the five estimates; (6) sanity-check that low/mode/high orderings and the annual-probability interpretation match the downstream model's expected parameterization.

issues.problem: Contradictory assumed states of the same control: R3's effectiveness is explicitly conditioned on Postgres Entra-auth being DEFERRED (Key Vault password fallback, 'NOT Entra-auth'), while R5's 65% effectiveness mode depends on 'Entra-only Postgres auth' being completed for ALL human accounts within the year. Both cannot be true; whichever is wrong shifts that risk's residual materially (R5's dominant kill-chain break disappears, or R3's residual secret-theft surface shrinks).
issues.suggested_correction: Fix a single canonical implementation state for Postgres authentication (and its timeline) at the portfolio level, then re-condition both effectiveness estimates on it. If Entra-auth is deferred, lower R5 effectiveness mode to roughly 50-55%; if it lands, raise R3's mode a few points and note the shared dependency in the downstream model.

issues.problem: Double counting of the shared infostealer precondition: R3's baseline chain embeds P(>=1 dev-laptop infostealer infection) of 35-60% as its dominant input, and R4's baseline is built on the same infostealer/loader foothold on the same ~60-laptop, local-admin, no-EDR fleet. A single infection event can be monetized as either a PAT/secrets harvest (R3) or ransomware (R4); the downstream 1-prod(1-p_i) formula then counts the same underlying event twice, inflating the aggregate.
issues.suggested_correction: Model endpoint infection as one shared upstream node and partition monetization outcomes conditionally (P(secrets-harvest path | infection) vs P(ransomware path | infection) summing to <=1), or explicitly deduct the overlap from one of the two baselines (e.g., reduce R4 mode by the share of infections already consumed by R3's chain, roughly 3-5 points).

issues.problem: Coverage gap between scope boundaries: R1 excludes endpoint-resident token/PRT/session-cookie theft as 'R4 territory', but R4 is scoped strictly to endpoint-origin RANSOMWARE/extortion. An infostealer stealing an Entra session cookie that leads to non-ransomware cloud data exfiltration (M365/SharePoint PII download) falls into neither baseline, so the aggregate understates this plausible material path — the very path R1 cites as its main residual.
issues.suggested_correction: Assign the endpoint-token-theft-to-cloud-exfiltration path explicitly to one risk (most naturally broaden R4 to 'endpoint-origin compromise' or add it back to R1's baseline with a few points), and record the assignment as a stated assumption in both estimates so the aggregation layer can verify no gap or overlap.

issues.problem: R4 and R5 received identical panel likelihood scores (3.5), yet R5's baseline mode (8%) is roughly half of R4's (15%) — and R5's own basis lists unusually severe aggravators (standing contractor Postgres logins to the crown-jewel dataset, six unlinked revocation points, zero detection). The divergence from panel parity is not reconciled anywhere; either the panel's likelihood rationale or the estimate is wrong.
issues.suggested_correction: Either raise R5's mode toward ~12-15% (consistent with the ex-employee-incident survey range of 10-25% the basis itself cites, given the aggravators) or add an explicit assumption documenting why the desk estimate departs from the panel's likelihood parity with R4 (e.g., low departure count dominates), so the discrepancy is a decision, not an accident.

issues.problem: The 65% effectiveness mode is internally inconsistent with R5's own stated residuals: pre-departure exfiltration by a soon-to-leave contractor — explicitly part of the named baseline scenario ('contractor exports tables after a dispute') — is admitted to be completely unmitigated, SCIM covers only 4-6 of ~15 apps, GitHub PATs survive to expiry, and the Entra-auth dependency conflicts with R3 (see companion issue). If pre-departure insider exfil is even ~25-30% of the baseline mass, arithmetic caps achievable effectiveness near 55-60%.
issues.suggested_correction: Decompose the R5 baseline into (a) post-departure dormant-credential use and (b) pre-departure exfiltration, state the assumed split, and recompute: effectiveness ~80% on (a) and ~0-10% on (b) yields a blended mode of roughly 50-58%, not 65%.

issues.problem: Ownership of the contractor direct-PostgreSQL exposure is assigned inconsistently across three estimates: R2 caps its RLS credit pending 'R3's contractor-account cleanup', R3 gates its Postgres network lockdown behind 'the R5 contractor-access work', and R5 treats contractor Postgres logins as its own core scenario. If each estimator assumed another package fixes it, the exposure may be mitigated in zero packages while three effectiveness estimates take partial credit for its removal.
issues.suggested_correction: Assign the contractor-DB-access remediation to exactly one package (R5 is the natural owner) with a committed timeline, then strip conditional credit from the other two estimates unless that timeline precedes their measurement window; add a cross-reference assumption to all three.

issues.problem: Each of the five effectiveness modes (70/80/65/65/65) is conditioned on ITS OWN multi-month package being 'fully implemented AND operated for the year' by the same 3-person IT team (no security engineer) that is simultaneously delivering the other four packages plus SOC 2 Type I (~20% of two sysadmins per R4). The conditions are individually plausible but jointly infeasible — total implementation load far exceeds team capacity — so taking all five modes at face value systematically overstates portfolio-level risk reduction.
issues.suggested_correction: Either (a) have the downstream model draw effectiveness from correlated distributions with a shared 'execution capacity' factor (with joint realization biased toward each estimate's low end), or (b) sequence the packages explicitly and use partial-year/low-end effectiveness for whichever packages land in the back half of the year.

issues.problem: Joint calibration failure: the five baseline modes (30/20/20/15/8) under the model's independence formula imply ~65% annual probability of at least one material incident, well above the ~20-45% all-vector SMB material-incident rate that R2's own basis cites as a ceiling ('R2 is only one vector, so its slice must be well below that'). Part of this is the double counting flagged above, but even after dedup the set likely sits above the survey ceiling without anyone having sanity-checked the aggregate.
issues.suggested_correction: Run the aggregate check explicitly: compute 1-prod(1-mode_i), compare to a below-average-maturity-adjusted all-vector benchmark (perhaps 35-55% for this control posture), and proportionally shade baselines — or document why Meridian's posture justifies exceeding the surveyed ceiling — before the deterministic model consumes these numbers.

issues.problem: Every one of the five baselines is load-bearing on the same unstated assumption: that unauthorized access to the ~500k end-user PII records 'near-automatically' triggers a notification duty and hence materiality. No estimate states the assumed jurisdictional mix of the data subjects (US-state vs GDPR/UK vs other), the controller-vs-processor question (as a B2B processor, Meridian's duty may be contractual notice to 1,200 customer orgs rather than regulator notification), or whether contractual SLAs alone cross $25k. If materiality-given-access drops from ~90% to ~50%, all five baselines drop nearly in half simultaneously.
issues.suggested_correction: Add a shared, explicit assumption block (used by all five estimates) specifying the data-subject jurisdiction mix, Meridian's processor obligations, and the assumed P(material | unauthorized PII access); have counsel validate it once rather than five separate human-review items.

correlation_concerns: Shared endpoint-compromise driver: the same infostealer infection on the unmanaged, local-admin, no-EDR laptop fleet is the dominant input to R3 (PAT/secret harvest), the primary vector for R4 (ransomware), and a residual path in R1 (session-cookie theft). Treating these as independent double counts single events, biasing the aggregate P(at least one material incident) UPWARD — probably the largest single distortion in the 1-prod(1-p_i) result.

Shared identity weakness: phishable push MFA with no Conditional Access underlies R1's ATO baseline, R3's phished-Entra-portal residual, and R5's credential-reuse-against-dormant-accounts scenario. These baselines co-move — if R1's phishing-resistant MFA package lands, R3 and R5 baselines both drop (R5's own assumptions admit this) — so the risks are functionally coupled through a common control, not independent.

Common-cause detection failure: no SIEM, no EDR monitoring, and business-hours-only triage by 3 people is the single mechanism that converts incidents into MATERIAL incidents in all five bases. This positive correlation means the independence formula overstates P(any incident) but materially UNDERSTATES the joint tail — the probability of multiple simultaneous or cascading material incidents in the same bad year, which is what actually bankrupts a $20M ARR company.

Correlated mitigation under-delivery: all five effectiveness estimates are conditioned on full implementation by the same 3-person team also running SOC 2. Execution slippage is a common-mode failure — if one package stalls, all likely stall — so residual risks should be modeled with a shared execution-quality factor rather than five independent point-estimate reductions; independence here biases the modeled residual portfolio risk DOWNWARD.

Deterministic cross-package dependencies stated by the estimators themselves: R2's effectiveness is conditioned on contractor-DB cleanup landing, R3's Postgres lockdown is gated on R5's work, and R3 vs R5 assume opposite states of Postgres Entra-auth. These are functional dependencies between residuals, so P(breach) = 1 - prod(1 - p_i) over residuals is structurally wrong for this set regardless of parameter values.

Net direction: for the headline annual P(>=1 material incident), the independence assumption biases the number UP (shared events counted in multiple risks); for tail/joint outcomes and for realized effectiveness, it biases risk DOWN (correlated detection failure and correlated implementation slippage). A decision-maker using the headline number sees inflated frequency but dangerously understated worst-case correlation.
redteam.overall_assessment: The set is unusually well-disciplined in ways red teams normally have to demand — every estimate flags LLM-memory provenance, none has an effectiveness mode above 80%, ranges are appropriately wide, and cross-risk ordering broadly tracks the panel (R1 30% > R3/R2 20% > R4 15%), with the one unreconciled exception that R5 (8%) sits at half of R4 despite identical panel likelihood of 3.5. The material defects are structural, not calibration-cowboy: (1) the five scope boundaries do not tile the event space — the infostealer precondition is counted in both R3 and R4, endpoint-cookie-theft-to-cloud-exfil falls into neither R1 nor R4, and the contractor Postgres exposure is claimed/deferred by three different estimates, with R3 and R5 assuming contradictory states of the same Entra-auth control; (2) the effectiveness estimates are individually conditioned on full implementation but jointly infeasible for a 3-person IT team also delivering SOC 2, so portfolio-level risk reduction is overstated; (3) nobody sanity-checked the aggregate — the modes imply ~65%/yr P(>=1 material incident) under independence, above the all-vector SMB ceiling R2 itself cites; and (4) all five baselines lean on one unstated shared assumption (unauthorized PII access near-automatically triggers notification-duty materiality, despite Meridian being a B2B processor) whose failure would halve every number simultaneously. Recommended before the deterministic model consumes these: define a canonical control-implementation timeline and a shared event taxonomy (dedup rule) at the portfolio level, restructure aggregation around shared upstream nodes (endpoint infection, identity weakness, detection absence, execution capacity) instead of five independent Bernoullis, and get one counsel-validated materiality assumption instead of five ad-hoc ones. The independence formula as specified will overstate the headline frequency while understating correlated worst-case outcomes — both errors matter for the prioritization decision this feeds.
